nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef slopes, lagoons, and back-reef zones; commonly found encrusting on rocky substrate and dead coral skeletons in moderate-light, moderate-flow environments

decorPreferences: spacing from neighboring corals (5–8 cm minimum)

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae. Has been known to benefit from occasional supplemental feeding of small zooplankton (rotifers ~0.5 mm, copepods, cyclops 1–2 mm) or amino acid solutions a few times per week. Target feeding via pipette during low-flow periods or after lights out tends to produce better uptake results.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: corals placed within sweeper tentacle reach (less than 5–8 cm)
aggressive LPS corals with long sweepers (frogspawn, torch, hammer)

imageCandidates.notes: Thomas Mesaglio; ~1024x768. Cyphastrea chalcidicum: vivid neon-green encrusting colony in situ, whole colony in frame, sharp, star-shaped corallites clearly visible — the most striking, colourful specimen in the pool.
Philippe Bourjon; ~1024x811. Cyphastrea serailia: large lobed tan colony, whole colony well-lit in shallow reef, sharp — best whole-colony framing for the genus.
Damien Wallace; ~1024x771. Cyphastrea ocellina: orange-brown encrusting colony on rock, whole colony visible, corallites in focus — adds a distinct species and warm colour.
Debra Baker; ~1024x768. Cyphastrea decadia: yellow-tan corallites against dark substrate with red sponge accent, sharp — represents a distinct species with colour contrast.
Ewout Knoester; ~1024x768. Cyphastrea microphthalma: brown encrusting colony in situ, whole colony framed — included for species coverage; water slightly turbid so lower-ranked.
Thomas Mesaglio; ~1024x768. Cyphastrea chalcidicum: tighter green corallite view, very sharp polyp detail — second angle showing the star-shaped corallite structure.
Ewout Knoester; ~1024x768. Cyphastrea serailia: greenish-tan encrusting colony on sand, whole colony in frame — second serailia angle/colour.
Debra Baker; ~1024x768. Cyphastrea decadia under bluish light: yellow polyps on dark substrate — second decadia angle with reef-lighting colour.

summary: Cyphastrea is an encrusting stony coral offered in a wide range of vivid color morphs and is generally considered one of the more approachable stony corals for reef keepers. It tolerates moderate light and is more forgiving of water-parameter variation than many other stony corals.
careNotes:
Cyphastrea is a genus of roughly 12 recognized species now placed in the family Merulinidae, having previously been classified under Faviidae before a major revision of stony coral taxonomy. The aquarium trade typically sells them at the genus level as 'Cyphastrea sp.' because husbandry requirements are near-identical across species. Common trade varieties include the 'Meteor Shower' morph — recognizable by its blue corallites with orange-red centers — along with various solid-colored encrusting forms.

Although Cyphastrea's individual polyps are small and resemble those of small-polyp stony (SPS) corals, the coral is broadly categorized and sold as a large-polyp stony (LPS) coral due to its fleshy tissue coverage and comparatively forgiving water-parameter tolerances. Care requirements are closer to those of intermediate LPS corals than to true SPS.

Lighting: A starting PAR (photosynthetically active radiation, a measure of usable light) of 100–150 is broadly recommended, with tolerance extending to around 250 PAR once the coral has acclimated. New frags should be placed in lower, shadier positions and moved upward very slowly — Cyphastrea tends to bleach under sudden high-light exposure. Increasing PAR by no more than 25–50 units per week is the generally advised acclimation pace.

Flow: Direct high-flow currents can prevent polyp extension and cause tissue damage. Flow should circulate around the colony rather than blasting it directly, which is especially important for smaller frags.

Aggression: Cyphastrea tends to extend sweeper tentacles at night that can sting neighboring corals. Keeping at least 5–8 cm of clear space between the colony edge and any neighboring coral is generally advised.

Water quality: Calcium, alkalinity, and magnesium supplementation — delivered via calcium reactor, kalkwasser, or two-part dosing — is required for sustained skeletal growth. Stable alkalinity is particularly important; large swings can stress the colony even if average values remain within range. Moderate nutrient levels (nitrate 5–10 ppm, phosphate 0.01–0.05 ppm) have been reported to support healthy coloration, while ultra-low-nutrient systems may cause browning.

Growth: Cyphastrea is generally regarded as a fast-growing encrusting coral. Reports indicate 5–10 cm of lateral expansion per year under good conditions, and colonies can cover significant areas of rock or aquarium glass over time. Fragging is straightforward with a bandsaw.
breedingNotes: Fragging is the standard captive propagation method. Colonies can be cut with a tile saw or bandsaw; frags readily encrust and grow on frag plugs or rubble substrate. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural reefs, synchronized to lunar and tidal cycles, but deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria — CCGC Aquacultured Red and Orange Cyphastrea Coral

additional.name: Tidal Gardens — Cyphastrea Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked calcium (~425 ppm), alkalinity (8–9 dKH), magnesium (~1350 ppm), lighting (under 100 PAR start, up to 150 medium), flow (moderate indirect), native range (Australia, Indonesia). Care level described as beginner-friendly / one of the easiest stony corals.

additional.name: Reef Chasers — Cyphastrea Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (77–84°F; outlier high end not adopted), pH (8.0–8.4), alkalinity (8–9 dKH), calcium (400–450 ppm), PAR (100–200). Care level: beginner. Confirmed semi-aggressive sweeper tentacle behavior.

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics — Cyphastrea Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (75–82°F), salinity (1.023–1.025), calcium (420–450 ppm), alkalinity (8–9 dKH), magnesium (1350–1450 ppm), PAR (50–200). Confirms moderate indirect flow and 2–3 inch sweeper-tentacle spacing requirement.

additional.name: Build Your Aquarium — Cyphastrea Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Indo-Pacific, Red Sea, East Africa, Australia, Philippines), temperature (72–78°F), salinity (1.023–1.025), alkalinity (9–12 dKH), calcium (350–450 ppm), magnesium (1200–1350 ppm). Notes sweeper tentacles as 'very short reach' compared to acan lords and frogspawn.
